success_criteria.description: All five districts are in use and every precinct is assigned.
All five districts have roughly equal population (within 10%).
Ken Party wins every seat — all 5 districts.
How skewed is your map? Mean-median gap ≤ 10%.

character.name: You
character.role: Ken Party Redistricting Director, Lakeview County
character.motivation: Last cycle, the Ryu Party won a seat in Lakeview by concentrating their voters along the lakeshore corridor. The Ken Party wants that seat back — and every other seat too. Your job: split the corridor so Ryu voters can't form a majority anywhere.

intro_slides.heading: The Corridor
intro_slides.body:
A narrow band of Ryu voters runs through the center of Lakeview County — along the lakeshore, where the apartments are dense and the politics lean hard Ryu.

Right now, if those voters are in one district, they win it. Your predecessor let that happen. You won't.

intro_slides.heading: The Cracking Play
intro_slides.body:
Cracking is the opposite of packing. Instead of concentrating the opposition, you split them. Draw district lines that cut through the corridor, dividing Ryu voters across multiple districts.

In each district, the corridor's Ryu precincts are outnumbered by the surrounding Ken territory. Ryu voters still vote — but they lose everywhere.

intro_slides.heading: The Disappearing Voice
intro_slides.body:
Notice what happens: a community that could win one seat now wins zero. Their total vote count hasn't changed. Their turnout hasn't dropped. But the lines moved, and their voice vanished.

This is what cracking does. It doesn't silence voters — it dilutes them until they don't matter.

narrative.objective: Crack the Ryu corridor across all 5 districts. Ken Party must win every seat.
